definition: Measure of force of blood against arterial walls
rationale: Blood pressure measures the force exerted by blood on arterial walls. Systolic is pressure during contraction diastolic is pressure during relaxation. Normal adult BP is approximately 120/80 mmHg.

definition: Pressure when heart contracts
rationale: Systolic pressure is the higher number measured when the heart contracts pumping blood into arteries. It reflects cardiac output and arterial resistance. Normal adult systolic is around 120 mmHg.

definition: Pressure when heart relaxes between beats
rationale: Diastolic pressure is the lower number measured when the heart relaxes between beats. It reflects peripheral vascular resistance. Normal adult diastolic is around 80 mmHg.

definition: Palpable arterial beat reflecting heart contractions
rationale: Pulse is the palpable expansion of an artery with each heartbeat. It provides information about heart rate rhythm and quality. Common sites include radial carotid and brachial arteries.

definition: Number of breaths per minute
rationale: Respiratory rate is the number of complete respiratory cycles breaths per minute. Normal adult rate is 12-20. Rate quality and effort should all be assessed.

definition: Amount of air moved with each breath
rationale: Tidal volume is the amount of air inhaled and exhaled with each normal breath approximately 500 mL in adults. It affects adequacy of ventilation along with respiratory rate.

definition: Oxygen saturation measured by pulse oximetry
rationale: SpO2 is the percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen measured non-invasively by pulse oximetry. Normal is 95-100%. Below 94% indicates hypoxemia requiring supplemental oxygen.

definition: Time for color to return after pressing on nail bed
rationale: Capillary refill time assesses peripheral perfusion. Press on nail bed release and count seconds for color to return. Normal is less than 2 seconds. Delayed refill suggests poor perfusion.

definition: Scale assessing level of consciousness
rationale: AVPU is a rapid consciousness assessment. A equals Alert V equals responds to Verbal stimuli P equals responds to Painful stimuli U equals Unresponsive. It is simpler than GCS for rapid assessment.

definition: Standardized scale measuring consciousness using eye verbal and motor responses
rationale: GCS scores eye opening verbal response and motor response on a scale of 3-15. It provides more detailed consciousness assessment than AVPU and is used for trending and communication.

definition: Visual examination of the patient
rationale: Inspection is the first step of physical examination using observation to gather information. Look for symmetry skin color deformities respiratory effort level of consciousness and other visual findings.

definition: Examination by touch
rationale: Palpation uses touch to assess temperature texture moisture movement masses tenderness and crepitus. Use appropriate pressure and watch the patient's face for pain response.

definition: Examination by listening usually with stethoscope
rationale: Auscultation uses a stethoscope to listen to body sounds including lung sounds heart sounds bowel sounds and blood pressure. It requires a quiet environment and practice to interpret.

definition: Examination by tapping to assess underlying structures
rationale: Percussion involves tapping on the body surface to assess underlying structures by the sounds produced. It can detect fluid air or solid masses. Used on chest and abdomen.

definition: Crackling or grinding sensation felt on palpation
rationale: Crepitus is a crackling or grinding sensation felt or heard indicating air under the skin subcutaneous emphysema or bone fragments rubbing together fracture. It is always an abnormal finding.

definition: Jugular vein distension indicating elevated venous pressure
rationale: JVD is visible distension of the jugular veins in the neck when the patient is at 45 degrees indicating elevated central venous pressure. It suggests right heart failure tension pneumothorax or cardiac tamponade.

definition: Excessive sweating
rationale: Diaphoresis is profuse sweating often associated with stress pain sympathetic activation or certain medical conditions. Combined with other symptoms it can indicate MI hypoglycemia or shock.

definition: Bluish discoloration from low oxygen in blood
rationale: Cyanosis is bluish discoloration of skin and mucous membranes caused by deoxygenated hemoglobin. Central cyanosis lips tongue indicates serious hypoxemia. Peripheral cyanosis may indicate poor circulation.

definition: Abnormal paleness of skin
rationale: Pallor is abnormal paleness resulting from reduced blood flow or reduced hemoglobin. It may indicate anemia shock vasoconstriction or syncope. Check mucous membranes in dark-skinned patients.

definition: Yellow discoloration from elevated bilirubin
rationale: Jaundice is yellowing of skin and sclera from elevated bilirubin caused by liver disease bile duct obstruction or excessive red cell breakdown. It indicates hepatic or biliary pathology.

definition: Mnemonic for patient history components
rationale: SAMPLE stands for Signs and symptoms Allergies Medications Past medical history Last oral intake Events leading to illness or injury. It provides organized approach to history gathering.

definition: Mnemonic for assessing pain or chief complaint
rationale: OPQRST assesses symptoms: Onset Provocation/Palliation Quality Region/Radiation Severity Time. It provides thorough assessment of pain or other chief complaints.

definition: Primary reason patient called for help
rationale: The chief complaint is the primary symptom or concern that prompted the call for help. It should be documented in the patient's own words when possible and guides the focused assessment.

definition: Relevant finding that is absent
rationale: Pertinent negatives are significant findings that are NOT present such as denies chest pain or no shortness of breath. They help rule out conditions and demonstrate thorough assessment.

definition: When symptoms began
rationale: Onset refers to when symptoms began and how they started suddenly vs gradually. Sudden onset may indicate different conditions than gradual onset even for similar symptoms.

definition: High-pitched whistling sounds from narrowed airways
rationale: Wheezing is a high-pitched musical sound produced by air flowing through narrowed airways. It is characteristic of asthma bronchospasm and airway obstruction. Usually heard on expiration but can be inspiratory.

definition: Crackling sounds from fluid or secretions in airways
rationale: Crackles or rales are discontinuous popping sounds heard when air moves through fluid or secretions in small airways. They indicate pulmonary edema pneumonia or other fluid accumulation.

definition: High-pitched inspiratory sound from upper airway obstruction
rationale: Stridor is a high-pitched harsh sound heard during inspiration caused by upper airway obstruction. Causes include croup epiglottitis foreign body and anaphylaxis. It indicates significant airway compromise.

definition: Low-pitched rumbling sounds from secretions in large airways
rationale: Rhonchi are low-pitched continuous sounds caused by secretions in larger airways. They may clear with coughing. Often heard in bronchitis and COPD with mucus production.

definition: Reduced or quiet lung sounds
correct_answer: Diminished breath sounds are quieter than normal and may indicate pneumothorax pleural effusion obesity or poor respiratory effort. Compare both sides noting any asymmetry.

definition: Pupils Equal Round Reactive to Light and Accommodation
rationale: PERRLA documents normal pupil assessment: both pupils are equal in size round in shape and react to light and accommodation focusing. Deviations may indicate neurological problems or drug effects.

definition: Dilated pupils
rationale: Mydriasis is pupil dilation. Bilateral mydriasis may result from medications stimulants cardiac arrest or brainstem dysfunction. Unilateral mydriasis may indicate herniation or direct eye trauma.

definition: Constricted pupils
rationale: Miosis is pupil constriction. Bilateral miosis is characteristic of opioid use organophosphate poisoning and pontine hemorrhage. Bright light also causes physiologic miosis.

definition: Unequal pupil sizes
rationale: Anisocoria is asymmetry in pupil size. Small differences may be normal but significant anisocoria especially with other neurological findings suggests serious pathology like herniation or CN III palsy.

definition: Mnemonic for trauma assessment findings
rationale: DCAP-BTLS helps remember what to look and feel for during trauma assessment: Deformities Contusions Abrasions Punctures/Penetrations Burns Tenderness Lacerations Swelling.

definition: How trauma occurred and forces involved
rationale: Mechanism of injury describes how traumatic injuries occurred including type and direction of force speed height falls and other factors. It helps predict injury patterns and guides assessment.

definition: Awareness of potential injuries based on mechanism
rationale: Index of suspicion is anticipating injuries that may be present based on mechanism even if not immediately apparent. High-energy mechanisms warrant high suspicion for occult injuries.

definition: Initial rapid assessment for life threats
rationale: Primary survey is the initial rapid assessment identifying and treating immediate life threats following ABCDE: Airway Breathing Circulation Disability Exposure. It precedes detailed secondary survey.

definition: Detailed head-to-toe examination after primary survey
rationale: Secondary survey is the systematic head-to-toe examination performed after life threats are addressed. It identifies all injuries and guides treatment priorities. Includes detailed physical exam and history.

definition: Critical time period for trauma patient treatment
rationale: The golden hour concept emphasizes that trauma patients have the best outcomes when they receive definitive surgical care within approximately one hour of injury. It drives rapid assessment transport decisions.

definition: Following changes in patient condition over time
rationale: Trending involves tracking vital signs and assessment findings over time to identify improvement or deterioration. Serial measurements are more valuable than single readings for identifying changes.

definition: Patient's normal status for comparison
rationale: Baseline refers to the patient's normal condition before illness or injury. Comparing current status to baseline helps identify changes. Ask family about normal status especially for elderly or disabled patients.

definition: Repeated evaluation to monitor patient status
rationale: Reassessment involves repeating assessment components to monitor patient response to treatment and identify changes. Frequency depends on patient acuity. Document reassessment findings.

definition: List of possible conditions matching patient's presentation
rationale: Differential diagnosis is the list of conditions that could explain the patient's signs and symptoms. Assessment aims to narrow the differential guiding treatment. EMS rarely makes definitive diagnoses.

definition: Process of using assessment to guide treatment decisions
rationale: Clinical decision making uses assessment findings patient history and protocols to determine appropriate treatment and transport decisions. It requires integrating information and critical thinking.

definition: Rapid pediatric assessment using appearance work of breathing and circulation
rationale: The PAT provides rapid across-the-room assessment of pediatric patients using three components: Appearance Work of Breathing and Circulation to skin. Abnormality in any suggests a sick child.

definition: Soft spot on infant skull where bones have not fused
rationale: Fontanelles are soft membranous gaps between infant skull bones. The anterior fontanelle is palpable until about 18 months. Bulging suggests increased ICP while sunken suggests dehydration.

definition: Unusual symptom pattern often seen in elderly
rationale: Atypical presentation occurs when patients especially elderly do not show classic symptoms of a condition. Elderly may have MI without chest pain or infection without fever. Maintain high suspicion.

definition: Taking multiple medications
rationale: Polypharmacy is the use of multiple medications common in elderly patients. It increases risk of drug interactions adverse effects and medication-related emergencies. Document all medications thoroughly.

definition: Presence of multiple medical conditions
rationale: Comorbidity refers to having multiple chronic medical conditions simultaneously. Elderly often have several comorbidities that affect their presentation response to treatment and baseline status.
